Yes, it is possible to use a different `create_app` function when running your Flask application as a server versus when running CLI commands. This can be achieved by leveraging the `app` object and the `flask` command-line interface (CLI) in a way that allows you to distinguish between the two contexts. You can also pass parameters to `create_app` when running as a CLI command without needing to set an environment variable, by using the `--app` flag or other CLI options.

You can achieve this by defining a custom `create_app` function that accepts a configuration dictionary, and then using that function to create the app instance. When running the Flask server, you can pass the configuration to `create_app` and use the resulting app object. When running CLI commands, you can pass the configuration to `create_app` as a parameter, and then use the resulting app object.

The key is to use the `app` object and the `flask` CLI to differentiate between the server and CLI contexts. For example, when running the server, you can pass the configuration to `create_app` and use the resulting app object. When running CLI commands, you can pass the configuration to `create_app` and use the resulting app object.

You can also use the `--app` flag when running the CLI command to pass the configuration to `create_app`. This way, you don't need to set an environment variable, and you can still control the app configuration based on the context.

In addition to this, you can pass parameters to `create_app` when running as a CLI command. For example, you can pass a configuration dictionary with a key-value pair that indicates whether the app was run as a CLI command. This way, you can determine the context of the app and use it to set the appropriate configuration.

The `create_app` function can be designed to accept a configuration dictionary, and you can use that dictionary to set the app's configuration. This allows you to pass different configurations for the server and CLI contexts.

You can also use the `flask` CLI to run commands with different configurations. For example, you can run `flask run` with a specific configuration, or `flask my_command` with a different configuration.

By using the `app` object and the `flask` CLI, you can control the app's configuration based on the context in which it is run. This allows you to have different configurations for the server and CLI commands.
